serving.natural_language_portion: A typical dim sum serving of steamed spare ribs is approximately 120 g, comprising 3 to 4 bone-in rib pieces served in a bamboo steamer basket.

nutrient_source.title: Steamed spare ribs — Singapore HPB Foodo Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record cr_id F06100044 is sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo with confidence 0.95 and review_status reviewed. All per_100g values are taken directly from the HPB record.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for steamed spare ribs with black bean sauce. GI estimated at approximately 35 (low) based on analogous mixed pork and legume-sauce dishes. The dish is predominantly protein and fat with only 7.18 g carbohydrate per 100 g, primarily from the black bean marinade and cornstarch. Low GI is consistent with high-protein, moderate-fat, low-carbohydrate mixed dishes as documented in Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care). Glycemic load per 120 g serving = (35 × 7.18 × 1.2) / 100 ≈ 3.0, classified as low GL.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 412 mg per 100 g from soy sauce and fermented black bean marinade
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to no more than 80 g per serving and request reduced soy sauce and fermented black bean in preparation, as a standard 120 g serving delivers approximately 494 mg sodium, approaching one-third of the 1500 mg daily limit recommended by the American Heart Association.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate (7.18 g per 100 g) with 5.69 g sugar, partially offset by 2.36 g dietary fibre and high protein (14 g per 100 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes can consume steamed spare ribs in moderate portions (80–100 g) as the high protein content slows gastric emptying and attenuates postprandial glucose rise, but should avoid additional sweet dipping sauces that increase the sugar load.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat approximately 4.8 g per 100 g and sodium 412 mg per 100 g; pork spare ribs are a red meat with arachidonic acid content
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit intake to small portions (60–80 g) and choose preparations with reduced soy sauce; the saturated fat and sodium content both contribute to cardiovascular risk, and frequent consumption of red meat is associated with elevated LDL cholesterol per AHA dietary guidelines.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Pork is a red meat rich in arachidonic acid (omega-6) which amplifies pro-inflammatory eicosanoid pathways; sodium 412 mg per 100 g may worsen RA-related tissue oedema
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should minimise or avoid steamed spare ribs as the arachidonic acid load from pork red meat amplifies inflammatory pathways, and the high sodium from fermented black bean sauce can exacerbate joint swelling; substituting with steamed chicken or fish is preferred.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Steamed spare ribs with black bean sauce (豉汁蒸排骨, Chi Zhi Zheng Pai Gu) is a classic Cantonese dim sum dish widely consumed in Singapore and across the Chinese diaspora. The dish consists of bite-sized pork spare rib pieces marinated in fermented black beans (douchi), minced garlic, soy sauce, sesame oil, ginger, and a small amount of cornstarch, then steamed in a bamboo basket until tender. The result is succulent, moist pork with a savoury, umami-rich flavour profile and a slightly soft texture from the steaming process. The fermented black beans impart a distinctive pungent depth that is characteristic of Cantonese cooking. Nutritionally, per 100 g the dish provides 202 kcal, 14 g protein, 13.08 g fat (approximately 4.8 g saturated), 7.18 g carbohydrate, 5.69 g sugar, 2.36 g dietary fibre, 412 mg sodium, 50 mg calcium, and 3.21 mg iron. The high sodium content (412 mg per 100 g) is a primary clinical concern for hypertension and cardiovascular disease management. The moderate carbohydrate load with significant protein makes it suitable for type 2 diabetes in controlled portions. As a red meat dish, it is not recommended for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis due to arachidonic acid content. In Singapore, this dish is commonly served at dim sum restaurants and hawker centres as part of a shared meal, typically for breakfast or brunch.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F06100044, review_status reviewed, confidence 0.95). This is an approved primary regional source for Singaporean dishes. The exact dish is present in the HPB database, qualifying for high confidence.
notes: saturated_fat_g value of 4.8 is estimated from USDA FoodData Central pork spare rib data (USDA SR Legacy item 10124) as HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this entry; this is documented as an adapted value. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1 as these values are not reported in the HPB Foodo entry for this dish and detailed micronutrient breakdown for this specific Singaporean preparation is not available in USDA FoodData Central or HPB as of 2026. GI is an analogous estimate (approximately 35, low) as no direct GI measurement for steamed spare ribs with black bean sauce exists in the University of Sydney GI Database; estimate is based on the low carbohydrate content (7.18 g per 100 g) and high protein-fat ratio consistent with low-GI mixed pork dishes. Input dietary_tags contained 'plant_based' which is incorrect for a pork dish and has been removed. Input occasion_tags contained only 'breakfast' which has been expanded to reflect typical dim sum meal contexts.
